food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dish with mock meat (plant-based protein)

preparation_method: dum cooking (slow steam cooking in sealed pot)
main_ingredients: mock chicken (soy protein or textured vegetable protein)
whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)
allergens: gluten (possible in some mock chicken formulations)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mock chicken dum biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice layered with mock chicken pieces, served with raita or curry sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani (composite dish, vegetarian adaptation) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central soy protein and basmati rice data for mock-chicken variant estimation
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mock-chicken vegetarian dum biryani exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani and chicken biryani entries, with the chicken component replaced by textured soy protein (TVP) data from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174276 — Soy protein isolate, and FDC ID 168875 — Basmati rice, cooked). The resulting composite per-100g profile reflects a typical restaurant-style vegetarian dum biryani with mock chicken. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice biryani and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian mock-chicken dum biryani. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice biryani (GI ~58, medium category) as the closest analogous food. The mock-chicken component (soy TVP) has a negligible GI contribution. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 49.8, where available carbohydrate per 350g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100g; ~1085 mg per restaurant serving of 350g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200g per meal, as a full restaurant serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~58) and high glycemic load per standard serving (~49.8 GL units)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions (150–200g) and pair it with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the medium GI basmati rice base and large carbohydrate load per restaurant serving can cause significant blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~1.8g per 100g) and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose versions prepared with vegetable oil rather than ghee to reduce saturated fat intake, and limit portion size to manage the combined sodium and caloric load, as a full serving contributes approximately 18g of fat and over 600 kcal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mock Chicken Dum Biryani (Vegetarian) is an Indian-origin rice dish served at Indian vegetarian restaurants in Singapore, notably at Jewel of India at Boat Quay. It is a plant-based adaptation of the classic dum biryani, replacing chicken with mock chicken made from textured soy protein (TVP) or similar plant-based protein. The dish is prepared using the traditional dum cooking method, in which layers of par-cooked spiced basmati rice and marinated mock chicken are sealed in a heavy pot and slow-cooked over low heat, allowing the steam to infuse the rice with aromatic spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, saffron, cumin, coriander, and turmeric. The result is a fragrant, moist, and richly spiced rice dish with a tender mock-meat texture. Nutritionally, a 350g restaurant serving provides approximately 602 kcal, 23.8g protein, 18.2g fat, and 85.75g carbohydrate, making it a calorie-dense, high-carbohydrate meal. The medium glycemic index (~58) and high glycemic load (~49.8) per serving make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (~1085 mg per serving) is clinically relevant for hypertension management. The dish is suitable for lacto-vegetarians and is commonly served with raita (yogurt-based condiment) or a side curry. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Indian vegetarian dining scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plate of vegetarian biryani?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for vegetarian mock-chicken dum biryani exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, USDA). Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani and chicken biryani entries with the chicken component substituted by USDA TVP data. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (basmati rice biryani) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN biryani entries and USDA FoodData Central soy protein data; no direct approved-source entry for mock-chicken vegetarian dum biryani exists. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA source data for this dish type. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA data for plant-based soy protein and vegetable oil preparation; confirmed zero for the rice component. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived B12 source; confirmed zero by formulation (no meat, no fortified ingredients assumed). 6. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice biryani from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. 7. Serving size of 350g is estimated from typical Indian restaurant biryani portion norms; actual restaurant portion may vary. 8. Halal status is not confirmed as mock chicken ingredients and preparation at the specific restaurant have not been independently verified. 9. Gluten-free status is not confirmed as some mock chicken products contain wheat gluten. 10. The food_name spelling 'Mock Chiken Dum Briyani' in the input has been corrected to 'Mock Chicken Dum Biryani' as the canonical English form.
